lines.text: Chapter one, section four. Proving an Equilibrium.
The driving example was intuitive enough that the equilibria were pretty obvious — of course both going right or both going left are stable solutions.
But Brokos points out that in more complicated scenarios, it won't always be so clear, and we need a systematic method for proving or disproving whether a given pair of strategies is an equilibrium.
That method follows directly from the definition.
Recall that a set of strategies is an equilibrium if and only if no single player can unilaterally change their strategy and get a better payoff.

So to prove that a particular set of strategies is an equilibrium, you take each player one at a time, hold everyone else's strategies fixed, and ask: is there any change that this one player could make to improve their own payoff?
If the answer is no for every player, you've proven it's an equilibrium.
If the answer is yes for even one player, you've proven it's not.
Let's walk through this with the driving game.
Start with the proposed equilibrium where both drivers go to the right.
Hold Driver Two's strategy fixed — they're going right.
Now look at Driver One's options.
Driver One can go right, getting a payoff of one.
Driver One can go left, getting a payoff of negative ten.
Driver One can go down the middle, getting a payoff of negative ten.
The payoff of one from going right is the highest available.
There is no deviation that improves Driver One's outcome.
Now hold Driver One's strategy fixed — they're going right — and examine Driver Two's options.
By the same reasoning, Driver Two gets one for going right, negative ten for any other choice.
No deviation helps Driver Two either.
Therefore, both-go-right is proven to be an equilibrium.
The book also illustrates how to use this method to prove that something is not an equilibrium.
Consider what happens if Driver One goes right while Driver Two goes left.
Both players get negative ten — they've collided.
Now, is this an equilibrium?
To find out, hold Driver Two's strategy fixed — they're going left — and ask whether Driver One can do better by changing.
If Driver One switches from right to left, they go from negative ten to positive one.
That is a strictly better outcome achieved by a unilateral change.
That single finding is enough — we don't need to check Driver Two.
The pair 'Driver One right, Driver Two left' is not an equilibrium.
And of course the same logic applies from Driver Two's side: if Driver Two switches from left to right while Driver One stays right, Driver Two also improves from negative ten to positive one.
Both players have incentive to deviate, confirming this is not an equilibrium.
One crucial point the book emphasizes: the test is always about unilateral deviation.
It doesn't matter whether both players changing their strategies simultaneously could lead to a better outcome — that's a different question entirely.
The equilibrium test asks what a single player can do on their own, holding everything else constant.
This might seem like a fine distinction right now, but it becomes very important when we move to more complex scenarios.

Sometimes a pair of strategies is not ideal in some global sense — both players might do better if they both changed — but it's still an equilibrium because neither player can improve their own situation acting alone.
In the Going to the Movies scenario coming up next, we'll see exactly that situation play out.
The discipline of checking each player's options one at a time, always keeping the other players' strategies fixed, is the core technique for equilibrium analysis throughout this book.
